candidates.title: Selective scope proof
candidates.claim: Agent proves it satisfies a required permission predicate without revealing the full permission set to the resource server, in a way that no configuration of RFC 7662 (plus jwt-introspection-response, RFC 8693, RFC 8707, DPoP) can match.
candidates.gap_to_close: RFC 7662 allows AS-side policy to return minimal scope per RS; JWT introspection response lets RS verify offline. Must find a property these fundamentally cannot express — candidates: runtime-adaptive predicate over permissions (not fixed at issuance), AS-blind presentation (no AS roundtrip, agent chooses what to disclose at the moment of use), constant-size proof regardless of bitmask width, adversarial-AS model where AS cannot lie about scope membership.
scenarios: regulated agent with 2^64 permission space where AS-side policy tables do not scale
AS is semi-trusted and RS needs cryptographic assurance independent of AS cooperation

candidates.title: Cross-scope unlinkability
candidates.claim: Same agent accessing different RS instances produces cryptographically unlinkable authorizations even under adversarial AS that tries to correlate per-agent traffic graphs.
candidates.gap_to_close: Needs formal security definition (IND-UNL-AS game), prototype demonstrating nullifier separation per scope, empirical test showing colluding AS+RS cannot de-anonymize, treatment of side channels (timing, nonce freshness).
scenarios: cross-credit-union member agent where CU-as-AS must not see member merchant graph
healthcare agent delegation across providers without issuer learning referral network

candidates.title: Delegation audit without exposure
candidates.claim: An auditor verifies that a delegation chain narrowed monotonically without reconstructing intermediate scopes or participants, in a form usable beyond narrow regulatory niches.
candidates.gap_to_close: Currently too narrow. Must broaden: chain-of-custody proofs for AI agent pipelines (where each hop is a tool call, not an org), whistleblower-safe agent delegation, cross-org agent handoff. Need formal semantics of narrowing proof and in-circuit enforcement.
scenarios: multi-tool AI pipeline where auditor wants proof that no hop exceeded its mandate without learning the mandates
journalist/source agent chain where intermediate nodes must stay hidden from auditor

candidates.title: Issuer-blind attribute predicates
candidates.claim: Agent proves a predicate over credential attributes (e.g. chartered_by_NCUA == true) without the verifier learning which issuer signed, with constant-size proof and arbitrary-schema support.
candidates.gap_to_close: Needs formal proof of issuer-hiding (IND-ISS game), constant-size predicate circuit that handles arbitrary Boolean expressions over claim schemas, benchmark showing BBS+/W3C VC cannot match without comparable circuit.
scenarios: cross-country KYB proof where jurisdiction must stay hidden

candidates.title: Bolyra as MCP auth, generally
candidates.claim: Bolyra strictly dominates vanilla OAuth 2.1 + MCP baseline (as shipped by Auth0, WorkOS, Stytch, Cloudflare Access) in the general case, not just regulated niches.
candidates.gap_to_close: Round 2 ruled this out. Must find a primitive that is both (a) outside vanilla OAuth's expressive envelope AND (b) needed by default in agent-economy MCP, not only in regulated scenarios.

hypotheses.label: Mutual ZK handshake
hypotheses.claim: Bolyra atomically proves human (Groth16) + agent (PLONK) identity bound to a session nonce. Vanilla MCP passes bearer token; no mutual identity proof; no human-in-the-loop binding.

hypotheses.label: Cryptographic binding of model instance to tool call
hypotheses.claim: Bolyra binds {model_hash, operator_pk, permission_bitmask} to a specific RS invocation. Vanilla OAuth binds only client_id; runtime model identity is not authenticated.

hypotheses.label: Zero-config identity portability across MCP servers
hypotheses.claim: One Bolyra credential authenticates at every compliant RS without per-RS client registration. Vanilla OAuth requires per-RS registration or RFC 7591 dynamic registration roundtrip.

hypotheses.label: Post-compromise resilience via forward-secure nullifiers
hypotheses.claim: Agent secret exfiltration does not retroactively deanonymize prior sessions. Vanilla bearer tokens have no forward secrecy.

hypotheses.label: Agent-economy-native delegation
hypotheses.claim: Narrow-scope delegation is a primitive (single proof per hop). Vanilla OAuth uses RFC 8693 token exchange requiring full AS roundtrip per hop.

scenarios: generic Claude agent talking to third-party MCP server via Anthropic connector
cross-vendor agent handoff (Claude → ChatGPT agent accessing same tool)

candidates.title: Cryptographic model-instance binding
candidates.claim: Bolyra cryptographically binds a specific tool call to a named model instance and operator — e.g. proves 'this call was made by Claude Sonnet 4.6 operated by ACME Corp under permission bitmask X' — without revealing the underlying API key, session token, or runtime secret to the verifier. No vanilla OAuth/MCP auth can bind runtime model identity to the message, because OAuth client_id identifies only the registered application, not which model/operator actually made the call.
candidates.gap_to_close: Must prove: (a) binding is non-malleable — attacker with an Opus key cannot forge a proof saying 'Sonnet made this call'; (b) binding survives key rotation — operator can rotate API keys without breaking historical attestations; (c) verifier learns ONLY {model_hash, operator_pk, permission_bitmask, message_hash} and nothing else. Must prove RFC 8693 token exchange and DPoP cannot achieve (a)+(c) simultaneously: either AS sees every hop (violates (c)) or binding is not cryptographic (violates (a)).
scenarios: Enterprise CISO needs to prove to auditor 'only approved models (Sonnet, Opus) touched PII files, never unapproved deployments' without revealing which call was which model
AI-agent liability dispute: bank claims agent drained account; Bolyra proves which specific model+operator made the transaction without revealing the operator's full session history
Tiered pricing verification: Anthropic wants to prove to a tenant that their Opus usage was actually Opus (not re-billed Sonnet) without exposing the tenant's full audit log
Model provenance for regulated AI: FDA/EU AI Act demands provable chain from deployed model weights to each inference output; OAuth cannot attest model identity

candidates.title: Forward-secure agent delegation
candidates.claim: If an agent's signing key is compromised at time T, sessions and delegations executed before T remain cryptographically unlinkable and non-replayable. No bearer-token or DPoP-bound OAuth construction achieves this — compromise of the key retroactively reveals the agent's prior activity graph and allows replay.
candidates.gap_to_close: Must define: (a) an IND-FS-AGENT game where adversary receives the key at time T, sees all prior transcripts, and must still fail to link or replay pre-T sessions with non-negligible advantage; (b) a concrete forward-secure nullifier construction using epoch-based key derivation that is efficient in PLONK (<10k constraints per epoch rotation); (c) proof that RFC 9449 DPoP cannot match — DPoP binds access tokens to keys but compromise of the key reveals every DPoP proof the agent ever generated, including the auth header chain.
scenarios: Long-running 30-day autonomous Claude agent whose key leaks on day 31 — prior 30 days of tool calls must stay private and non-replayable
Security researcher finds leaked agent key in a public repo; without forward secrecy they reconstruct full activity history and replay sessions; Bolyra prevents this
Model rotation for compliance: ACME rotates all agent keys quarterly; prior-quarter sessions must stay pseudonymous under new keys (key rotation ≠ forward secrecy; both needed)
Whistleblower agent: source uses agent to relay leaks; if agent is seized, past communications must stay unlinkable to source
